name: Lough Derg
description: Lough Derg, located in County Donegal, Ireland, is a lake steeped in religious and mythological significance. It is most famously associated with St. Patrick's Purgatory, a pilgrimage site on Station Island within the lake. The island is said to be the location where St. Patrick received a vision or revelation that allowed him to see the entrance to Purgatory. For centuries, pilgrims have journeyed to Lough Derg to undertake a rigorous three-day pilgrimage of prayer, fasting, and penance, seeking spiritual purification and atonement for their sins. The pilgrimage involves enduring sleep deprivation, fasting, and praying barefoot while walking around designated stations on the island. The experience is considered a profound spiritual challenge and a deeply transformative experience for those who undertake it. Beyond its Christian associations, the lake and its surrounding landscape are intertwined with older, pre-Christian beliefs and folklore. Some believe the area holds ancient Celtic spiritual energies and that the lake itself may have been considered a sacred place long before St. Patrick's arrival. The remote and stark beauty of Lough Derg contributes to its mystique and reinforces its reputation as a place where the veil between the earthly and spiritual realms is thin. The pilgrimage continues to draw thousands of people annually, solidifying Lough Derg's enduring importance as a sacred site in Irish religious and cultural history. Its unique blend of Christian penitential practice and echoes of older Celtic traditions makes it a compelling and enigmatic location.
subtitle: Gateway to Purgatory and spiritual renewal

keyMyths.title: St. Patrick's Vision
keyMyths.description: St. Patrick received a vision revealing the entrance to Purgatory on Station Island in Lough Derg. This vision led to the establishment of the pilgrimage site, attracting penitents for centuries.

keyMyths.title: The Cave of Purgatory
keyMyths.description: A cave on Station Island was believed to be the actual entrance to Purgatory, where pilgrims could experience the torments of the afterlife. This cave was eventually sealed, but the pilgrimage continues.

sources.author: Shane Leslie
sources.work: Saint Patrick's Purgatory: A Record from History and Literature
sources.description: A comprehensive historical and literary examination of Lough Derg and its association with St. Patrick's Purgatory.
sources.text: A comprehensive historical and literary examination of Lough Derg and its association with St. Patrick's Purgatory.

sources.author: Richard Kearney
